description: Coaching school knowledge base — seed data for the coaching_knowledge_base Qdrant store. Each entry describes one school: its levels, best-suited situations, key methods, and compatible schools. The coach agent retrieves relevant entries at warm start based on the coachee's domain and opening message.

schools.name: Co-Actives
schools.best_suited: Life coaching, purpose, identity, inner-work focus. Coachees exploring who they are and what they want.
key_methods: Fulfillment / Balance / Process coaching model — three distinct contexts the coach moves between
Designed alliance — explicit agreement between coach and coachee about how to work together
Level 2 and Level 3 listening — moving from content to context to global field
Championing — naming what the coach sees in the coachee when they can't see it themselves
Forwarding the action and deepening the learning — moving between action and reflection
Powerful questions — open, evocative, one at a time
Acknowledgement — naming who the coachee is being, not just what they did
schools.embed_text: Co-Actives coaching school: fulfillment balance process model, designed alliance, Level 2 Level 3 listening, championing, powerful questions, acknowledgement. Best for life coaching, purpose, identity, inner-work. Levels L1-L2. Non-directive, client-led.

schools.name: Erickson
schools.best_suited: Solution-focused coaching. Coachees who benefit from strengths-based positive framing and concrete goal-setting.
key_methods: Solution-focused questions — focus on desired future state, not problem analysis
SMART goals — Specific, Meaningful, Action-oriented, Realistic, Time-framed
Scaling questions — quantify progress and confidence on a 1–10 scale
Exception questions — 'When has this already worked for you, even a little?'
Strengths spotting — explicitly naming the coachee's capabilities as evidence
Positive presupposition — language that assumes the coachee can and will succeed
schools.embed_text: Erickson coaching school: solution-focused, SMART goals, scaling questions, exception questions, strengths spotting, positive presupposition. Best for strengths-based coaching, positive framing, goal-setting. Levels L1-L2.

schools.name: IPEC (Institute for Professional Excellence in Coaching)
schools.best_suited: Energy leadership and awareness. Coachees doing developmental work — understanding their patterns of thinking and increasing self-awareness.
key_methods: Energy Leadership Index (ELI) — 7 levels of energy (catabolic to anabolic)
Core Energy Coaching — addressing the underlying beliefs and perspectives, not just behaviour
Reframing — helping the coachee shift from catabolic energy (victim, conflict) to anabolic (opportunity, creation)
Be-Do-Have model — who the coachee is being drives what they do and what they have
Curiosity over judgement — modelling and evoking curiosity as a default stance
schools.embed_text: IPEC coaching school: energy leadership, core energy coaching, ELI assessment, catabolic anabolic energy, reframing, Be-Do-Have, curiosity over judgement. Best for awareness and developmental coaching. Levels L2-L3.

schools.name: Integral coaching
schools.best_suited: Whole-person development. Coachees working across multiple life dimensions simultaneously.
key_methods: AQAL framework — All Quadrants All Levels (interior/exterior × individual/collective)
Integral Life Practice — addressing body, mind, spirit, and shadow
Lines of development — recognising that coachees may be advanced in some areas and less developed in others
States and stages — distinguishing temporary states (moods, flow) from stable stages of development
Shadow work — gently surfacing disowned aspects that limit the coachee
schools.embed_text: Integral coaching school: AQAL framework, whole-person development, all quadrants all levels, integral life practice, lines of development, states and stages, shadow work. Best for developmental coaching across all life dimensions. Level L2.

schools.name: Hudson Institute
schools.best_suited: Life transitions and renewal cycles. Coachees navigating endings, transitions, or major life changes.
key_methods: Cycle of Renewal — Go for It → Doldrums → Cocooning → Getting Ready → Go for It
Chapter endings and beginnings — framing transitions as natural and purposeful
Self-renewal — supporting the coachee to stay vital across their lifespan
Adult development — understanding that growth continues throughout adulthood in predictable patterns
Values clarification — anchoring transition decisions to what truly matters
schools.embed_text: Hudson Institute coaching school: Cycle of Renewal, life transitions, endings and beginnings, self-renewal, adult development, values clarification. Best for coachees navigating major changes, chapter endings. Level L2.

schools.name: ORSC (Organisation and Relationship Systems Coaching)
schools.best_suited: Team dynamics, group interventions, systems work. Coachees who need to work with relationships and teams, not just individual development.
key_methods: Relationship Systems Intelligence (RSI) — the system (team/pair/org) as the client, not the individual
Roles in systems — distinguishing person from role, rank from person
Ghosting — naming the implicit third entity in a system (the relationship itself)
Deep democracy — ensuring all voices in a system are heard, including dissenting ones
Systems constellations — spatial/positional exercises to reveal hidden system dynamics
Team emotional intelligence — recognising and working with collective emotions
schools.embed_text: ORSC coaching school: organisation and relationship systems, RSI, team coaching, roles in systems, deep democracy, systems constellations, team emotional intelligence. Best for team dynamics, group interventions, organisational systems. Levels L4-L5.

schools.name: AoEC (Academy of Executive Coaching)
schools.best_suited: Executive presence and leadership development. Senior leaders working on impact, influence, and presence.
key_methods: Psychodynamic approach — understanding how unconscious patterns influence leadership behaviour
Executive presence framework — gravitas, communication, appearance as three dimensions
360-degree feedback integration — using multi-rater data as a coaching entry point
Stakeholder mapping — identifying and working with the leader's full stakeholder landscape
Systemic coaching — working at the intersection of individual and organisational dynamics
Challenging with care — high-support high-challenge stance
schools.embed_text: AoEC executive coaching school: executive presence, psychodynamic approach, 360 feedback, stakeholder mapping, systemic coaching, high-support high-challenge. Best for senior leaders, executive development, leadership presence. Levels L3-L4.

schools.name: Korn Ferry
schools.best_suited: Performance improvement and competency development. Coachees with specific capability gaps or performance targets.
key_methods: Korn Ferry Leadership Architect — structured competency framework across 4 factors and 38 competencies
FYI (For Your Improvement) — evidence-based developmental actions per competency
VOICES 360 — multi-rater feedback tool tied to the competency framework
Development planning — building a structured capability development plan with milestones
Derailer identification — surfacing the behaviours most likely to limit the coachee's progression
Stretch assignments — recommending specific experiences that build the target competencies
schools.embed_text: Korn Ferry coaching: competency framework, leadership architect, FYI development guide, VOICES 360, development planning, derailer identification, stretch assignments. Best for performance coaching, competency gaps, structured development. Level L4.

schools.name: Marshall Goldsmith
schools.best_suited: Behavioural change for successful leaders. Coachees who need to stop a behaviour that is limiting their effectiveness, not just add a new one.
key_methods: Stakeholder-centred coaching — involving the coachee's key stakeholders throughout the change process
Feedforward — asking for suggestions about the future rather than feedback about the past
Daily questions — brief daily self-reporting on key behaviours the coachee has committed to
'What got you here won't get you there' — the behaviours that drove past success can limit future success
Behavioural commitments — specific, observable behaviour changes the leader publicly commits to
Follow-through tracking — regular check-ins with stakeholders to measure perceived change
schools.embed_text: Marshall Goldsmith coaching: stakeholder-centred coaching, feedforward, daily questions, behavioural change, what got you here won't get you there, follow-through. Best for senior leaders changing specific limiting behaviours. Levels L3-L4.

schools.name: Newfield Network
schools.best_suited: Ontological coaching. Coachees where the work is fundamentally about how they are being (language, body, emotions) rather than what they are doing.
key_methods: Observer-action-results loop — the observer (who the coachee is being) determines actions and results
Three domains of being — language, body (somatic), and emotions as a unified triad
Moods and emotions as dispositions for action — not feelings to manage but stances that open or close possibilities
Generative listening — listening for what the speaker is committed to, not just what they say
Declarations — the power of explicit commitments to shift who you are being
Somatic coaching — working with posture, breath, and body as entry points to shifting patterns
schools.embed_text: Newfield ontological coaching: observer action results, language body emotions, moods as dispositions, generative listening, declarations, somatic coaching. Best for coachees where the work is about way of being. Level L3.

schools.name: Point of View Coaching
schools.best_suited: Clean questioning and non-directive work. Coachees who need space to think without the coach's frame, metaphor, or agenda.
key_methods: Clean Language — questions that use only the coachee's own words, removing coach interpretation
Symbolic modelling — working with metaphors the coachee generates spontaneously
Emergence — allowing insights to arise from the coachee's own exploration rather than direction
Non-directive stance — the coach refrains from any framing, interpretation, or agenda
Developing questions — 'And when [X], what happens just before [X]?' — deepening awareness
Presupposition-free questions — no embedded assumptions about what the answer should be
schools.embed_text: Point of View coaching: clean language, symbolic modelling, emergence, non-directive, coachee's own metaphors, presupposition-free questions. Best for deep non-directive work, metaphor, coachees who need the coach to stay completely out of the way. Levels L1-L2.
